question: Identify the root cause of the network fault: which device, what changed, and why it breaks.
description:
R3 adds a TCP-MD5 password to its iBGP neighbor toward R2. R2 has no
password configured. The TCP layer drops R2's segments (MD5 option
required by R3); the BGP session never completes a 3-way handshake.
R2's session to R7 (secondary RR) is unaffected, so the prefix flow
partially survives. Subtle RR-A vs RR-B asymmetry — the session to one
RR is dead but the other works.
expected_rca:
Root cause: on R3, a TCP-MD5 password was added to the iBGP neighbor toward R2 while R2 has no password.
Because only one end authenticates the TCP segments with MD5, the two ends disagree on the TCP-MD5 option, so the underlying TCP connection cannot be established and the R3<->R2 BGP session never completes its handshake; R2's session to R7 (secondary RR) is unaffected, so prefix flow partially survives - an RR-A-dead / RR-B-alive asymmetry.
Fix: remove the password from R3's R2 neighbor.
expected_symptoms_summary:
BGP session state change (%ROUTING-BGP-5-ADJCHANGE):
- R2: neighbor 192.0.2.3.
- R3: neighbor 192.0.2.2 → a.
- R3: neighbor 192.0.2.4 → a.

%IP-TCP-3-NOAUTH reported by R2 (3 instances):
- R2: Authentication info not programmed for the packet received from 192.0.2.3:60077 to 192.0.2.2:179
- R2: Authentication info not programmed for the packet received from 192.0.2.3:60077 to 192.0.2.2:179
- R2: Authentication info not programmed for the packet received from 192.0.2.3:60077 to 192.0.2.2:179

%IP-TCP-3-BADAUTH reported by R3 (3 instances):
- R3: Invalid MD5 digest from 192.0.2.2:179 to 192.0.2.3:60077 for vrf:default (0x60000000)
- R3: Invalid MD5 digest from 192.0.2.2:179 to 192.0.2.3:60077 for vrf:default (0x60000000)
- R3: Invalid MD5 digest from 192.0.2.2:179 to 192.0.2.3:60077 for vrf:default (0x60000000)

apply.commands:
configure terminal
router bgp 65000
neighbor 192.0.2.2
 password clear secret

revert.commands:
configure terminal
router bgp 65000
neighbor 192.0.2.2
 no password
